lesson.title: Linear Transformations
lesson.body: A function T: Rⁿ → Rᵐ is a linear transformation if T(u+v) = T(u) + T(v) and T(cu) = cT(u) for all vectors and scalars. Every linear transformation is represented by a matrix: T(x) = Ax. The kernel (null space) of T is {x : T(x) = 0}, and the image (range) is {T(x) : x ∈ Rⁿ}. T is injective iff ker(T) = {0} and surjective iff image(T) = Rᵐ.

rooms.question: Which condition must a function T satisfy to be a linear transformation?
rooms.latex: T(\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{v}) = T(\mathbf{u}) + T(\mathbf{v}) \text{ and } T(c\mathbf{u}) = cT(\mathbf{u})
rooms.explanation: Linear transformations must preserve vector addition and scalar multiplication. A consequence is T(0) = 0.

rooms.question: The standard matrix of a rotation by 90° counterclockwise in R² is:
rooms.explanation: Rotation by θ has matrix [[cos θ, -sin θ], [sin θ, cos θ]]. At θ = 90°: cos90°=0, sin90°=1, giving [[0,-1],[1,0]].

rooms.question: The kernel of a linear transformation T: Rⁿ → Rᵐ is:
answers: {y ∈ Rᵐ : y = T(x) for some x}
rooms.explanation: ker(T) = null space = all input vectors that map to the zero vector. Its dimension is the nullity.

rooms.question: Match these properties to their definitions (order from most to least restrictive for T: Rⁿ → Rᵐ):
items: Linear map: preserves + and scalar mult
rooms.explanation: Isomorphism is the most restrictive (bijective). Surjection and injection are one-sided. Linear map is the base requirement.

rooms.question: For T: Rⁿ → Rᵐ with standard matrix A, T is injective iff:
rooms.explanation: T is injective iff ker(T) = {0} iff null space is trivial iff columns of A are linearly independent iff rank(A) = n.

boss.name: The Transformation Tyrant
boss.taunt: In my domain, every input has one output. But not every output has an input.

questions.question: For T: R³ → R² with rank(T) = 2, what is nullity(T)?
questions.explanation: Rank-Nullity Theorem: rank + nullity = n = 3. So nullity = 3 − 2 = 1.

questions.question: T: Rⁿ → Rⁿ is an isomorphism if and only if:
answers: Its standard matrix A is invertible
questions.explanation: For T: Rⁿ → Rⁿ, isomorphism ⟺ bijective ⟺ invertible standard matrix. T⁻¹(x) = A⁻¹x.

questions.question: If T and S are linear transformations with standard matrices A and B respectively, the standard matrix of T∘S is:
questions.explanation: (T∘S)(x) = T(S(x)) = T(Bx) = A(Bx) = (AB)x. Composition corresponds to matrix multiplication in the same order.
